_purpose: Courthouse Atlas for Hoke County, NC. Created from Robeson County in 1911 — records begin at that date. Earlier records for Hoke County families are in Robeson County.
display_name: Hoke County, North Carolina — Courthouse Atlas

previous_courthouses.fate: Hoke County was carved from Robeson County in 1911. All records before that date are filed in Robeson County. Lumberton is approximately 25 miles south of Raeford.

record_losses.cause: None — Hoke County has experienced no major courthouse fire or catastrophic record loss
records_survived: All major record series from 1911 onward
record_losses.description: Hoke County's records are intact from the county's formation in 1911. The 1911 boundary is the key research challenge — families in this area before 1911 are documented in Robeson County records.
record_losses.narrative_impact: For {ancestor_name}'s family, the Hoke County courthouse has records beginning only in 1911. Everything before that date is in the Robeson County courthouse in Lumberton.
record_losses.source: NC State Archives. Guide to County Records. Raleigh, 2001.

deeds.completeness: Complete from county formation. Pre-1911 deeds in Robeson County.
deeds.gaps: None in Hoke County records. The 1911 gap is jurisdictional.
deeds.repository: Hoke County Register of Deeds, Raeford; NC State Archives microfilm

wills_estates.repository: Hoke County Clerk of Court; NC State Archives

marriages.completeness: Complete from county formation. Three-race filing system applies.
marriages.repository: Hoke County Register of Deeds; NC State Archives microfilm

court_minutes.gaps: Pre-1911 court proceedings in Robeson County
court_minutes.repository: Hoke County Clerk of Court; NC State Archives

substitute_sources.description: All civil records for Hoke County families before 1911 are filed in Robeson County at the courthouse in Lumberton, NC.
substitute_sources.repository: Robeson County Courthouse, Lumberton, NC; NC State Archives microfilm
substitute_sources.era_coverage: 1787-1911 (Robeson County formation to Hoke County formation)
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Essential. No Hoke County substitute exists for this period.
substitute_sources.source: NC State Archives. Guide to County Records. Raleigh, 2001.

substitute_sources.description: Before 1920, Hoke County families were enumerated in Robeson County. The 1920 census is the first to enumerate Hoke County separately.
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Must trace from Robeson County census (pre-1920) to Hoke County census (1920+).

substitute_sources.description: Fort Bragg installation records, civilian employee records, and military service records for personnel stationed at the base in western Hoke County.
substitute_sources.repository: National Personnel Records Center, St. Louis; U.S. Army records at National Archives
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Relevant for families employed at Fort Bragg or who served there. Civilian employee records may document hometown, next of kin, and demographic information.
substitute_sources.source: U.S. Army Center of Military History.

narrative_hooks: Hoke County was carved from Robeson in 1911 — the courthouse in Raeford is younger than {ancestor_name}'s grandmother. Everything before 1911 is in Lumberton, twenty-five miles south, filed under the old county name.
If {ancestor_name}'s family was farming in what became Hoke County before 1911, the deed is in Robeson County's vault, not Hoke's — the county line moved, the records did not.
The 1920 census is the first that lists {ancestor_name}'s family under 'Hoke County.' Before that, the same family, the same farm, the same community — all filed under 'Robeson County.'
Fort Bragg absorbed the western third of Hoke County, and if {ancestor_name} worked at the base, the records are in the Army's files, not the county's.
source_references: NC State Archives. Guide to County Records in the North Carolina State Archives. Raleigh: Division of Archives and History, 2001.
Lefler, H.T., and A.R. Newsome. North Carolina: The History of a Southern State. 3rd ed. Chapel Hill: University of North Carolina Press, 1973.
U.S. Army Center of Military History. Fort Bragg installation history.
